shot.subject: water
shot.notes: the lake at midday, a sheet of moving light in the sculpted land

segments.text: Poured into the lowest lines of the Gardener's sculpture lies the strangest substance in the garden. We drink it, swim in it, take it entirely for granted — and nearly every property that makes life possible here traces back to its peculiar habits.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: the surface only: ripples interleaving, light fracturing and mending

segments.text: It is a simple thing: two hydrogen atoms bonded to one oxygen, a molecule bent at a slight angle. That small bend changes everything.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: skimming flight just above the surface, reflections streaming past

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: macro on wavelets: crests forming, breaking, reforming without pause

segments.text: That bent shape gives the molecule two faintly charged ends, so each one tugs on its neighbors. From that single fact, a cascade of consequences. Water clings to itself — cohesion — which is why it beads, why it climbs the thinnest root against gravity, and why its surface pulls taut enough for an insect to stand on. At ant scale that skin is a floor, strong enough to walk.

shot.subject: shore
shot.notes: water threading between grass stems, wetting each blade as it passes

segments.text: That same stickiness makes water the finest solvent the garden owns. It dissolves more kinds of substance than any common liquid — minerals, salts, gases, the nutrients a root needs. Almost nothing in this world travels except dissolved in water. It is the garden's bloodstream and its delivery service at once.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: cold light on the lake, the surface still, the deep water dark below

segments.text: Here is the strangest habit of all. Nearly every substance shrinks and sinks as it freezes. Water does the opposite — near freezing it expands, so ice is lighter than the liquid beneath it. That is why ice forms a lid on top of a pond rather than filling it from the bottom.

shot.subject: fish
shot.notes: beneath a chilled surface: fish holding in the darker, warmer deep

segments.text: Reverse that habit and the garden's winters would be lethal. Ponds would freeze solid from the floor up, and the fish, the giant, every creature of episode nine, would have nowhere to wait out the cold. Instead the ice roof insulates the water below, and life goes on in the dark. A single quirk of one molecule keeps the drowned country alive.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: sun blazing on the lake, a faint haze rising off the warm surface

segments.text: The water you see is only pausing here. It is caught in a wheel that never stops turning, powered by the sun overhead. Warmth lifts water off the lake as invisible vapor — evaporation — and the grass and trees breathe out still more through their leaves, a process called transpiration. The garden exhales moisture all day long.

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: moist air rising off the world, gathering pale in the cooler heights

segments.text: Up where the air is colder, that vapor cools and gathers back into droplets — condensation — building the clouds. And when the droplets grow too heavy to float, the sky hands the water back.

shot.subject: rain
shot.notes: rain striking the surface, each drop a crown and a ring

segments.text: Rain. Or, in the cold, snow. What falls on the high ground runs downhill across the surface as runoff, or soaks into the soil as infiltration, feeding the roots and the hidden water table before seeping, at last, back to the lowest place there is. Evaporate, condense, precipitate, return — the same water, going around forever. The lake is not a store. It is a stage the water is passing across.

shot.subject: shore
shot.notes: waves arriving at the beach edge-on, at ant scale enormous, working the sand

segments.text: Moving water does not only carry life. It carries the land itself. Every drop that runs downhill picks up a few grains and sets them down lower, and over enough time that patient theft is what carves valleys, rounds stones, and builds the shallows.

shot.subject: shore
shot.notes: following the waterline as it works along the border of soil and lake

segments.text: The Gardener shapes the ground with a hand; the water reshapes it without one, grain by grain, on its way to the sea it never reaches here. Where land and water meet, the border is always being renegotiated — the lake widening a whisper in the wet season, the shore reclaiming it in the dry. It is the slowest argument in the garden, and the water usually wins.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: from above: the lake's whole outline, shore to shore, in one frame

segments.text: Stand back and consider how little of this there is. Most of the world's water is salt, undrinkable to root and creature alike. The freshwater that every land life depends on is a slim fraction of the whole — and most of that is locked in ice or buried deep. The living world runs on the thin remainder that moves through lakes, rivers, soil, and air. This bright pond is a rare thing wearing an ordinary face.

shot.subject: fish
shot.notes: from below: motes and nutrients drifting down through green light toward waiting mouths

segments.text: And every creature is mostly made of it. A body is a clever way of keeping water in one place — using it to hold shape, to carry food and waste, to shed heat, to run every chemical reaction life depends on. The fish swims in water; the ant, the flower, the Visitor carry their own portion inside. To be alive in this garden is, mostly, to be water that learned to walk.

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: tilt up from the lake to the dome that lifts, cools, and returns its water

segments.text: Which points at the engine above it all. The water rises, gathers, and falls at the command of a single vast machine overhead — the same machine that lights the garden, times its days, and sends down every drop.

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: the full dome above the garden, clouds burning at the horizon
